items.title: Florida man goes on Amazon shopping spree with stolen debit card, buys door hinges and Axe products: FCSO
Ga. deputies foil suspected prison contraband drop, arrest Florida man
Florida man arrested for trying to steal a septic tank
items.comment: A Florida man decided the one thing missing from his life was someone else's septic tank, and set out to correct the oversight. Deputies caught up with him before the plan could reach its deeply unpleasant conclusion.

items.title: An HOA fined a neighbor for having too many packages on the front porch
items.comment: Someone on the HOA board looked at a porch with a few too many delivery boxes and reached, naturally, for the fine book. Nothing rattles a homeowners association quite like visible proof that people buy things.

items.title: 'Abe Lincoln' fined for reciting the Gettysburg Address at the Lincoln Memorial
items.comment: A man dressed as Abraham Lincoln was fined for standing at the Lincoln Memorial and reciting the Gettysburg Address. The National Park Service, it turns out, requires a permit for that, and Honest Abe did not have one.

items.title: Washington Post class action alleges customer data was used to set subscription prices
items.comment: A class action claims The Washington Post quietly used customers' own data to decide how much to charge each of them for a subscription. Democracy dies in darkness; your personalized price, apparently, is set there too.

items.title: Adobe reaches $150 million settlement over termination fees and subscription cancellations
items.comment: Adobe agreed to a $150 million settlement over claims it buried the cancel button and larded subscriptions with surprise termination fees. It turns out the hardest thing Adobe ever shipped was a way to actually leave.

items.title: Parents can be fined or charged for their kids' bad behavior in a South Jersey town
items.comment: A South Jersey town passed a rule letting it fine, and even charge, parents when their children misbehave in public. The plan to cure youthful mischief is, essentially, to bill the nearest available adult for it.

items.title: Florida man arrested after trying a TikTok challenge inside a Walmart
items.comment: A Florida man was arrested after attempting a TikTok challenge inside a Walmart, trading potential likes for an actual mugshot. The algorithm giveth engagement; the deputies taketh it away.

items.title: Florida man arrested after joyriding a lawnmower through a Target store
items.comment: A Florida man was arrested after joyriding a lawn mower through the aisles of a Target, cutting precisely nothing along the way. It was, at the very least, the most groundskeeping that Target's floors will ever receive.

items.title: Iowa restaurateur's lake house HOA dispute leaves him on hook for $1M-plus in legal fees
items.comment: An Iowa restaurateur's dispute with his lake-house homeowners association has left him staring down more than a million dollars in legal fees. The house was supposed to be where he relaxed; instead it became a seven-figure hobby.

items.title: 'Lazy Landlord List': Released by the City of Albuquerque to highlight fined landlords
items.comment: The city of Albuquerque decided the sharpest tool against negligent landlords was public humiliation, so it published an official 'Lazy Landlord List.' It is government by group-chat energy, now with a municipal masthead.

items.title: Disabled veteran fights homeowners association lawsuit over flagpole in Florida yard
items.comment: A disabled veteran in Florida is fighting his homeowners association in court over the radical act of putting a flagpole in his own yard. The HOA has decided that the real threat to the neighborhood is a man and his flag.

items.title: Man found to have violated Walla Walla's chalk-use ordinance, fined $5,000
items.comment: A Walla Walla man was found to have violated the city's chalk-use ordinance and fined five thousand dollars, a remarkable price for a sidewalk. Somewhere there is a municipal code that ranks washable chalk as a $5,000 offense, and a man who found it the hard way.

items.title: Arizona homeowner fined $100 repeatedly for giving out free water in the heat amid clash with HOA
items.comment: An Arizona man kept getting hit with $100 fines for the crime of handing out free water to people in the desert heat. His homeowners association looked at a neighbor giving away cold water in summer and saw, above all else, a violation.

items.title: A burglar broke into a Brooklyn home, kissed the tenant, and ran off
items.comment: He broke in, kissed the tenant, and fled into the night without taking a single thing — a burglary in form only. Detectives are now investigating a crime whose apparent motive is, simply, affection.

items.title: NYPD is looking into people who keep emerging from manholes at night
items.comment: Somewhere beneath the city, people keep climbing out of manholes after dark, and the NYPD would very much like to know why. So far the department has plenty of sightings, a growing case file, and absolutely no explanation.

items.title: A failing robot-cop company is now publishing AI fan-fiction about its own robots
items.comment: Unable to make its security robots actually fight crime, the struggling company has pivoted to publishing AI-generated fan-fiction in which they heroically do. It is the rare business plan where the product works flawlessly, provided it stays fictional.

items.title: Florida man stole Smokey Bear signs and tried to sell them on Facebook
items.comment: Only you can prevent forest fires; only he could look at the signs warning about it and see resale inventory. He pried loose the Smokey Bear placards and listed them online, which is precisely how the authorities found them too.

items.title: Florida man arrested for driving in reverse down a busy road
items.comment: Every road is technically one-way if you are committed enough to reverse down the entire length of it, and he was extremely committed. Police disagreed with the interpretation and arrested him heading, briefly, forward.

items.title: Homeowner faced $500-a-day HOA fines over an unapproved door
items.comment: Five hundred dollars a day, indefinitely, over a single door the homeowners association had not personally signed off on. It ultimately took a brand-new state law to switch the meter off.

items.title: HOA fines an Arizona man for keeping a cooler in his own driveway
items.comment: A man kept a cooler in his own driveway, and his HOA responded with fines, because nothing threatens property values quite like accessible cold beverages. The cooler stays put; the citations keep arriving.

items.title: A five-year HOA fight over a short-term rental could reach the state Supreme Court
items.comment: A single short-term rental has fueled a five-year legal war that may now climb all the way to the state Supreme Court. Somewhere, a system designed to keep neighborhoods calm is working exactly as intended.

items.title: A car ended up on a roof, and police had to explain how
items.comment: A car somehow ended up on top of a roof, forcing police to hold a straight-faced briefing to explain a sentence that should not exist. Physics did something dramatic; the roof, notably, was never consulted.

items.title: TSA issues a warning after a bizarre peanut butter incident at the airport
items.comment: The TSA issued a formal warning after a peanut butter incident strange enough to reach the national news. It spreads, it apparently sets off alarms, and it has now earned its very own airport advisory.

items.title: A lawyer took on his own HOA and ended up jailed for contempt
items.comment: He was an actual practicing attorney who took his own homeowners association to court, and somehow walked out jailed for contempt instead. He knew the law; the HOA knew the judge, and the HOA remains undefeated.

items.title: Florida man arrested for trespassing while wearing an actual jail outfit
items.comment: He was arrested for trespassing while already dressed, head to toe, in a genuine jail outfit, which is either terrible luck or excellent planning. Dress for the job you want; he was, at minimum, fully prepared for booking.

items.title: Florida man arrested for burglary on his way to court for a different burglary
items.comment: He was arrested for a fresh burglary while on his way to court to answer for a previous one, which is at least thematically consistent. Time management is hard, but he did technically make both appointments.

items.title: Florida man tries to lawyer his way out of a traffic stop — by pretending to be a lawyer
items.comment: He had no bar card, no firm, and no case — just the unshakable confidence of a man who decided to represent himself on the shoulder of the highway. The deputies, it turns out, were not persuaded by opposing counsel.

items.title: Family fined by their HOA for a Christmas lights display
items.comment: Nothing says peace on earth quite like a homeowners association citation for displaying too much holiday cheer. The lights stayed up, the neighbors stayed festive, and the invoice stayed firmly in the mailbox.
items.source_name: ABC News - Breaking News, Latest News and Videos

items.title: Florida man fights the deputies pulling him out of an 'alligator-infested' canal
items.comment: Deputies pulled him out of a canal they described as alligator-infested, and he thanked them by fighting the people doing the rescuing. He wanted to stay; the alligators, the deputies, and basic survival instinct all disagreed.

items.title: Restaurant fined for hiding its health alert behind a potted plant
items.comment: The health-code violation was bad enough, but the real artistry was concealing the official alert behind a single, strategically placed potted plant. Inspectors found both the violation and the houseplant defense thoroughly unconvincing.

items.title: A $100 HOA fine took years of court battles to resolve
items.comment: A hundred-dollar fine escalated into years of court battles and untold billable hours, all to resolve a sum you could find in a couch cushion. Everyone technically won, and the $100 remains completely undefeated.

items.title: A whale sinks a fire boat in Raritan Bay
items.comment: A whale collided with a fire boat in Raritan Bay and sent the vessel straight to the bottom, an outcome that is genuinely difficult to phrase in an incident report. The boat lost decisively, and the whale was never so much as questioned.

items.title: Town moves to fine parents when their kids break e-bike laws
items.comment: When kids break the local e-bike laws, the town's bold new plan is to hand the ticket to their parents instead. It's accountability, thoughtfully outsourced one full generation upward.

items.title: HOA fines residents for flying the American flag
items.comment: Residents were fined by their homeowners association for the deeply subversive act of flying the American flag on American soil. The most American thing in the whole story isn't the flag — it's the fine for the flag.

items.title: Florida man pulled over with 34 open White Claws on the passenger seat
items.comment: He was pulled over with thirty-four open White Claws riding shotgun, a figure that is neither casual nor easily explained to a deputy. Not one, not a sensible six — the passenger seat was clearly having a bigger night than he was.

items.title: Restaurant fined under a 'no dancing' law, launches a petition
items.comment: A restaurant got fined under an actual, still-enforced 'no dancing' ordinance and answered the only sensible way: by launching a petition. It's Footloose in real life, except the town wins the opening act and the fine is entirely, boringly real.

items.title: 'Cop Santa Claus' hauls in a pair of suspected Christmas tree thieves
items.comment: An officer in a full Santa Claus getup personally hauled in a pair of suspected Christmas tree thieves, which is almost too on-the-nose to be real. Santa now knows who's been naughty and, apparently, holds arrest powers; the trees were recovered.

items.title: Homeowner sues his HOA over $80,000 in fines, 'and counting'
items.comment: His homeowners association fines started small and quietly metastasized past eighty thousand dollars, so he is finally suing to make it stop. 'And counting' is doing a truly heroic amount of work in that sentence.

items.title: Florida man leads a chase, then drives his SUV straight into a pond
items.comment: He led police on a chase that was going reasonably well right up until he steered his SUV directly into a pond. The plan was flawless until the water; the SUV has filed no comment since.

items.title: Burglary suspect undone by a hilariously dumb escape error
items.comment: The burglary itself was nearly flawless, right up until an escape blunder dumb enough to carry the entire police write-up. The crime was almost perfect; the exit strategy very much was not.
